title: Removal of Surface Penetrant
narrationScript: Everything we have done so far put penetrant into flaws. Now we have to take the excess off the surface without pulling penetrant back out of the flaws, and that is a razor's edge. Leave too much on the surface and the part glows or stains everywhere, so real indications hide in the noise. Take off too much and you strip the flaw itself, and the indication never forms. We call those underwashing and overwashing, and a removal technician walks between them every shift. Start with water-washable penetrant, Method A. These penetrants carry a built-in emulsifier, so plain water rinses them away. That convenience is also the risk, because the same water that cleans the surface will happily keep going and clean out the flaws. So control the wash. Use a coarse droplet spray, never a solid needle jet. Keep the pressure modest, commonly no more than about forty pounds per square inch, with water between about fifty and one hundred degrees Fahrenheit. Hold the nozzle at an angle to the surface instead of blasting straight in. And on a fluorescent line, wash under ultraviolet light, because the glow is your gauge. You watch the background fade as you rinse, and the moment the surface background is gone, you stop. Washing past that point buys you nothing and costs you indications. Method C is the solvent wipe, the standard for field work with aerosol kits. The sequence matters. First wipe the surface with a clean, dry, lint-free cloth to take off the bulk of the penetrant. Then take a fresh cloth dampened, not soaked, with solvent remover, and wipe away the last traces until the surface is clean. What you never do is flood the surface with solvent or spray remover directly onto the part. Solvent poured over the surface runs into every flaw and dissolves the penetrant you spent ten minutes putting there. A dampened cloth cleans the surface and leaves the flaw alone. Post-emulsifiable penetrants split removal into two stages on purpose. The penetrant itself is not water-washable, so it stays put until you apply a separate emulsifier, and that gives you an engineered, controllable removal step for high sensitivity work. Method B uses a lipophilic emulsifier, an oil-based one. The part is dipped in emulsifier or the emulsifier is flowed over the surface, and from that moment a clock is running, because a lipophilic emulsifier works by diffusing into the penetrant layer. Do not agitate, do not brush it around, and do not stretch the contact time, because the longer it sits, the deeper it works, and given enough time it reaches the penetrant inside the flaws. When the qualified contact time is up, a water rinse stops the action and carries the emulsified layer away. Method D uses a hydrophilic emulsifier, a water-based detergent type, and it is the choice for most high sensitivity aerospace work. The sequence starts with a plain water pre-rinse that knocks most of the surface penetrant off before the emulsifier ever sees the part. Then the part goes into the emulsifier bath, typically diluted to somewhere between five and thirty-five percent for a dip tank, and much weaker for spray application, commonly five percent or less. The emulsifier acts on the thin layer that remains, the bath concentration is checked with a refractometer as part of process control, and a final water rinse finishes the job. More steps, more control, fewer stripped flaws. Whatever the method, know the failure signs. An overwashed part looks beautiful, clean and dark under the ultraviolet lamp, and that is exactly the problem, because a stripped flaw and a sound part look the same. If you know the wash ran long, or the emulsifier sat too long, stop. The fix is not a quick splash of fresh penetrant. The fix is to clean the part, dry it, and run the whole process again from the start. An underwashed part shows the opposite face, a haze of background glow or red smear that can bury an indication. Your procedure may allow a brief, controlled additional wash, and beyond that, you reprocess. Removal rewards a steady hand and punishes a hurried one. Wash to background, never past it.
imagePrompt: A technician in a darkened wash booth rinses a fluorescent-penetrant-coated turbine disk with a coarse fan of water spray under deep blue UV lamps, glowing green residue streaming off the part into the drain.

sections.heading: Selective Removal: The Razor's Edge
sections.body:
The removal step has one purpose: take the penetrant **off the surface** while leaving it **in the flaws**. Both failure directions destroy the test.

- **Underwashing** leaves residual surface penetrant. Under UV-A it appears as background glow; with visible dye, as a red haze or smear. Indications must compete with that noise, and fine ones lose.
- **Overwashing** keeps removing after the surface is clean - water, solvent, or emulsifier reaches into the flaws and strips the penetrant that capillary action spent the whole dwell placing. The part then looks perfect and inspects clean, which is precisely what makes overwashing the classic PT sin.

The removal technique is matched to the penetrant **Method**: A (water-washable), B (post-emulsifiable, lipophilic), C (solvent-removable), and D (post-emulsifiable, hydrophilic). Each has its own controls and its own way of going wrong, and your written procedure defines the parameters for all of them.

sections.heading: Method A: Water-Washable
sections.body:
Method A penetrants contain a **built-in emulsifier**, so plain water removes them directly - fast and economical for batches and rough surfaces, and the easiest method to overwash because nothing separates the removal agent from the flaw-entrapped penetrant.

Typical procedure controls:

- **Coarse droplet spray**, never a solid needle jet - droplets scrub the surface without driving water into flaws.
- **Pressure commonly ≤ 40 psi** at the nozzle.
- **Water temperature about 50-100 °F.**
- **Spray at an angle** to the surface rather than head-on, keeping the nozzle moving.
- **Minimum effective time:** wash until the background is gone, then stop.

On fluorescent lines the wash station has its own UV-A lamp, because the fading background glow is the endpoint gauge - you literally watch the wash finish. With visible penetrant, you watch the red surface film rinse to a faint, even blush and stop there. Dwell-to-wash timing, water pressure, and temperature all come from the procedure, and changes to any of them are process changes, not operator preferences.

sections.caption: Method A wash technique under UV-A - coarse angled spray at modest pressure, watching the background glow fade and stopping the moment it clears.

sections.heading: Method C: Solvent-Removable
sections.body:
Method C is the field technique that pairs with aerosol kits and nonaqueous developer, and its discipline is entirely in the wiping sequence:

- **Step 1 - dry wipe.** Remove the bulk of the surface penetrant with a clean, **dry, lint-free cloth**. Most of the excess comes off here, with zero risk to flaw-entrapped penetrant.
- **Step 2 - dampened wipe.** Lightly moisten a fresh cloth with solvent remover - **dampened, not dripping** - and wipe away the remaining traces until the surface is clean. Fold to a clean face often; under UV-A, a final check shows whether residual background remains.

What is **never** done: flooding the surface with solvent, spraying remover directly onto the part, or soaking the cloth until it streams. Free solvent flows into every discontinuity and dissolves the penetrant inside - an instant, invisible overwash. Solvent removal is slower per square foot than washing, which is why it suits local examinations rather than whole-part processing, but done correctly it is precise and leaves flaw penetrant untouched.

sections.title: Three removal rules that protect indications
sections.body:
1. **Wash to background, never past it** - the endpoint is a clean surface, not a timer run long.
2. **Never flood solvent onto the test surface** - bulk off with a dry wipe, finish with a solvent-**dampened** cloth.
3. **Emulsifier contact time is a stopwatch operation** - it keeps working the longer it sits, and given enough time it reaches the flaws.

sections.heading: Methods B and D: Engineered Removal with Emulsifiers
sections.body:
Post-emulsifiable penetrants are **not water-washable by themselves** - by design. Removal happens only where and when you apply an emulsifier, which makes these systems the controlled choice for high-sensitivity work on critical hardware.

**Method B - lipophilic (oil-based).** The emulsifier is applied by **immersion or flowing on** - not sprayed, not brushed - and it works by **diffusing into the penetrant layer** from the moment of contact. The part is not agitated, and the **contact time is critical**: long enough to emulsify the surface layer, short enough that diffusion never reaches the flaw-entrapped penetrant. A water rinse stops the action and carries the emulsified layer away.

**Method D - hydrophilic (water-based, detergent-type).** A plain **water pre-rinse** first strips most of the surface penetrant, reducing the load on the emulsifier. The part then enters the emulsifier - **dip concentrations typically 5-35%**, spray applications much weaker, **commonly ≤ 5%** - where the remover scrubs the remaining thin layer chemically. A **final water rinse** completes removal. Bath concentration is a controlled process variable, checked with a **refractometer**. The extra stages are the point: each one is separately controllable, which is why hydrophilic removal dominates high-sensitivity fluorescent inspection of critical aerospace parts.

sections.caption: Removal flow for the two post-emulsifiable methods - timed lipophilic emulsifier contact for Method B, and pre-rinse, controlled-concentration hydrophilic emulsifier, and final rinse for Method D.

sections.heading: Overwash, Underwash, and Recovery
sections.body:
Learn to read the wash result the way an inspector will:

- **Overwashed:** the surface looks flawless - dark and clean under UV-A, spotless with visible dye. Indications are faint, broken, or missing entirely, and a stripped flaw is indistinguishable from a sound part. Causes: washing past background, excessive pressure or temperature, needle-jet spray, flooded solvent, or emulsifier contact run long. **Recovery is total:** clean the part, dry it, and reprocess from penetrant application - re-applying penetrant briefly over a stripped surface is not a qualified shortcut.
- **Underwashed:** background glow or dye haze remains - speckled, smeared, or pooled in surface roughness. Real indications can hide inside it, and developer applied over heavy residue produces a noisy, uninterpretable result. Your procedure may permit a brief, controlled supplemental wash; beyond that, reprocess.

Because an overwashed part looks *good*, process discipline - pressure, temperature, time, technique, and lighting at the wash station - is the only real defense. The daily system performance check on a known-defect panel is what catches a wash that has quietly drifted destructive.

sections.caption: The same cracked part at three wash endpoints under UV-A - overwashed with the indication stripped, correctly washed with a crisp indication on a dark field, and underwashed with background glow masking the flaw.

figures.title: Post-emulsifiable removal flows (Methods B and D)
Overwash, correct wash, and underwash under UV-A

interactive.title: At the Wash Station
interactive.intro: You are the removal technician on a fluorescent penetrant line, with a solvent field kit on the bench for local jobs. Make the removal calls a Level II would make. Typical procedure values apply throughout - and the written procedure governs.

steps.prompt: A rack of water-washable (Method A) parts finishes dwell. Set up the rinse:
options: Needle jet, 80 psi, hot water - fastest wash wins
Coarse droplet spray at no more than about 40 psi, water around 50-100 °F, working at an angle under the UV-A lamp
Soak the parts in a tub of warm water until the glow disappears
Wipe each part with a solvent-soaked rag instead of rinsing
steps.feedback: Method A removal is a coarse, angled, modest-pressure spray watched under UV-A. Needle jets and high pressure drive water into flaws; soaking and solvent rags are uncontrolled removal.

steps.prompt: Mid-wash, most of the background is gone but a few smeared patches still glow. You:
options: Keep washing the whole part another five minutes to be safe
Raise the pressure to 80 psi on the stubborn patches
Continue the coarse spray briefly on the remaining patches only, and stop the moment the background clears
Wipe the patches with a solvent cloth and move on
steps.feedback: Wash to background, never past it - and treat only the areas that need it. Raising pressure or blanket-washing the whole part again is how overwashing happens; solvent on a water-washable surface is an uncontrolled process change.

steps.prompt: You catch yourself having sprayed a part well after its background cleared, and its known reference area now looks dim. The correct recovery is to:
options: Apply fresh penetrant for one minute, then re-wash gently
Proceed to developer - bleed-out will compensate
Double the developer dwell to amplify what is left
Clean the part, dry it, and reprocess from penetrant application onward
steps.feedback: Overwashing strips flaw-entrapped penetrant, and nothing downstream can recover it. A quick re-application is not a qualified fix - the part is cleaned, dried, and fully reprocessed.

steps.prompt: On the Method D line, a coworker suggests skipping the water pre-rinse to save cycle time since the emulsifier 'does the work anyway.' You refuse, because the pre-rinse:
options: Removes the bulk of the surface penetrant so the emulsifier works a thin, predictable layer and the bath is not overloaded
Cools the part below the temperature band
Is only cosmetic and could indeed be skipped
Replaces the final rinse at the end of the process
steps.feedback: The pre-rinse is what makes hydrophilic removal controllable: the emulsifier only has to handle a thin residual film, contact stays predictable, and bath life is protected. Skipping it is a process change, not a time-saver.

steps.prompt: A field call: one weld on a platform, visible penetrant, solvent technique (Method C). The dwell timer sounds. Your removal sequence is:
options: Spray solvent remover directly over the weld, then wipe dry
Wipe with a clean dry lint-free cloth first, then finish with a fresh cloth lightly dampened with remover
Flood the weld with water from a bottle, then towel it off
Leave the excess in place - developer will absorb it
steps.feedback: Method C is a two-step wipe: dry cloth for the bulk, then a solvent-dampened (never soaked) cloth for the traces. Spraying or flooding solvent onto the surface dissolves penetrant out of the flaw - instant overwash.

checkQuestions.question: Typical procedure controls for a Method A water wash are:
options: Coarse droplet spray, ≤ 40 psi, water about 50-100 °F, stop when background clears
Needle jet, 100 psi, water above 140 °F, wash for a fixed 10 minutes
Continuous soak in warm water until the part stops glowing
Any pressure, provided the water is cold
checkQuestions.explanation: Water-washable removal uses a coarse, angled spray at modest pressure and moderate temperature, with the endpoint set by the disappearing background - not a timer run long.

checkQuestions.question: In Method C removal, the correct sequence is:
options: Flood the surface with solvent, then blot dry
Wipe with a solvent-soaked cloth, then rinse with water
Dry lint-free wipe to remove the bulk, then a fresh cloth lightly dampened with solvent for the remaining traces
Apply developer first, then wipe everything off together
checkQuestions.explanation: The dry wipe takes off most of the excess risk-free; the dampened wipe finishes the surface. Free-flowing solvent enters flaws and dissolves the trapped penetrant, so flooding is prohibited.

checkQuestions.question: Why is contact time so critical for a lipophilic (Method B) emulsifier?
options: The emulsifier evaporates and stops working after a fixed time
It diffuses progressively into the penetrant layer, and with enough time it reaches and emulsifies the penetrant inside flaws
Short contact times damage the part surface
The emulsifier only works while the part is agitated
checkQuestions.explanation: A lipophilic emulsifier keeps diffusing deeper the longer it sits - the contact time is qualified to emulsify only the surface layer, and the part is not agitated. The water rinse stops the action.

checkQuestions.question: Typical hydrophilic (Method D) emulsifier concentrations are:
options: About 5-35% for dip tanks, and commonly 5% or less for spray application
Always used at full strength from the drum
About 50-75% for dip and 40% for spray
Below 1% in all cases
checkQuestions.explanation: Hydrophilic remover is diluted in water - typically 5-35% in immersion tanks and much weaker (commonly ≤ 5%) when sprayed - with bath concentration verified by refractometer as a process control.

checkQuestions.question: Why is an overwashed part more dangerous to the inspection than an underwashed one?
options: Overwashing leaves background that hides indications
Overwashing corrodes the part surface
An underwashed part cannot accept developer
An overwashed part looks clean and acceptable, but flaw penetrant has been stripped - missing indications give a false clean result
checkQuestions.explanation: Underwash announces itself as visible background noise; overwash hides itself as a beautiful clean surface with the evidence stripped out of the flaws. That false-clean outcome is why overwashing is the classic PT sin.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.4 (removal of surface penetrant)
ASTM E165 / ASTM E1417 (penetrant examination - general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (liquid penetrant examination - general practice)
